title: TDOA positioning: from equations to working code
preview: The gap between a clean least-squares multilateration formula on paper and a system that actually works in a noisy room is wider than expected...

detail.body:
Time-difference-of-arrival positioning looks elegant on paper. You have N speakers emitting beacon signals, you cross-correlate with a reference, find the peaks, compute time deltas, and solve a least-squares system. Done.

In practice: the peaks are not clean. Room reflections create secondary peaks that are sometimes larger than the direct-path peak. The sampling rate creates a quantization floor on TDOA resolution. Background noise shifts the correlation baseline.

What actually worked: averaging 8 consecutive cross-correlation windows before peak detection, using a minimum peak-height threshold relative to the correlation maximum, and running a sanity check that TDOA values are physically plausible given speaker geometry. After those three fixes, positioning error dropped from ~40 cm to ~6 cm RMS.
